items.title: Despite barely any population growth, Tasmania doesn't have enough houses

children.content: Tasmania is still battling rising homelessness and an ever-tightening rental market, despite having the slowest growing population in the country.

gallery.altText: A house model and a key.

thumbnail.altText: A house model and a key.

items.title: Shark bites diver in waters off Bruny Island

children.content: A shark has bitten a diver on the forearm in waters off Bruny Island, Tasmania.

image.altText: Broadnose sevengill shark.

items.title: Smelter clean-up bill likely to be funded by Tasmanian taxpayers, experts say

children.content: The clean-up bill for the Liberty Bell Bay manganese smelter site may ultimately may be paid by the Tasmanian government, according to legal experts, following administrators' failure to secure a buyer for the embattled operation.

image.altText: Worker inside a smelter factory.

items.title: How clogging and crafting saved a rural Tasmanian town hall from closure

children.content: Every Sunday, a group of men and women get together for some American clogging, home-baked afternoon tea and community connection.

image.altText: a group of women dance in tap shoes in an old wooden-walled building

items.title: Going after the media a tried and true tactic of diversion

children.content: A minister of the Crown's scolding of a journalist asking a legitimate question about firearm ownership, and the subsequent pile-on allowed to run on social media, should ring alarm bells for anyone concerned with democracy.

image.altText: A bearded man speaking at press conference.

avatar.alt: A woman with glasses stands in a TV studio.

items.title: England wins crazy 10-goal thriller against France to claim third

children.content: England claims third place at the World Cup in a crazy game against France, which finishes 6-4 and sees Kylian Mbappe become, for now, the World Cup's greatest ever goalscorer.

articles.title: Messi received like royalty in New York ahead of World Cup final

children.content: Argentina's Lionel Messi has received rock star reception in New York City ahead of the World Cup final against Spain, but how much of a lasting impact will this tournament make in a crowded sporting market?

image.altText: Lionel Messi winks and gives a thumbs up as he sits on a stage

articles.title: How an Italian sculptor created the World Cup trophy that became an icon

children.content: It has become the most iconic trophy in world sport, and it all started in a small design studio in Milan more than 50 years ago.

image.altText: A man places a trophy into a box

Declan Rice smiles and celebrates with Marcus Rashford

items.title: Pauline Hanson delivers CPAC London speech to mostly empty seats

children.content: Pauline Hanson has ended her trip to Britain and Europe on a muted note, after about 120 people attended her speech at a political conference in London on Saturday.

image.altText: Pauline Hanson wears a blue business dress on stage with a big screen behind her

items.title: Hanson faces rough political seas beyond the Mediterranean

children.content: Many voters are unhappy with the hand they feel successive governments have dealt them and no-one has tapped into that better than Pauline Hanson.

image.altText: Pauline Hanson speaks to reporters among a crowd, wearing a brown and black jacket.

items.title: The Aussie Leo who shares the same condition as Messi is rooting for Argentina

children.content: Lionel Messi was diagnosed with growth hormone deficiency, which is a rare condition, when he was younger. He has become a role model for young Leo Slattery from Brisbane.

gallery.altText: A young boy wearing an Argentina shirt.
Lionel Messi in the world cup match against England last week celebrating
The slattery family smile for a photo and Leo is wearing a Messi jersey

thumbnail.altText: A young boy wearing an Argentina shirt.
Lionel Messi in the world cup match against England last week celebrating
The slattery family smile for a photo and Leo is wearing a Messi jersey

items.title: As Europe follows Australia's social media ban, the US opts for own way

children.content: More and more countries are following Australia's lead and introducing social media bans for children, but US policies are piecemeal and vary from state to state.

image.altText: Alexis sits at an outdoor table with a metal fence and trees in the background.

items.title: Even where the population isn't booming, houses are in short supply

children.content: Despite having the slowest growing population in the country, Tasmania is still battling rising homelessness and an ever-tightening rental market.

image.altText: A house model and a key.

items.title: The Houdini of Boggo Road escaped the prison in 1946. He shouldn't have taken a friend

children.content: In 1946, Arthur 'Slim' Halliday recruited two associates, scaled the walls of Boggo Road Gaol for a second time, hopped in a taxi and vanished.

gallery.altText: A graphic collage of three prison escapees, a news article, and Boggo Road Gaol.

thumbnail.altText: A graphic collage of three prison escapees, a news article, and Boggo Road Gaol.

items.title: A US tech start-up wants to turn night into day. The question is why?

children.content: Reflect Orbital has won initial approval for a plan to put thousands of mirrors in orbit that would be visible from anywhere on Earth. But will anyone pay for sunlight?

image.altText: A large circle of sunlight illuminates a city at night

items.title: Quiet change to Nauru policing powers could thwart legal challenges

children.content: Advocates question the timing of law changes that coincide with mounting federal court challenges from individuals fighting deportation.

image.altText: A Nauru man in a black police officers shirt, stands with his hands on a desk.

items.title: Australia's only full-time penny-farthing maker is breaking world records

children.content: Farmer-turned-professional penny-farthing maker Dan Bolwell has a cult following of international enthusiasts.

gallery.altText: Dan Bolwell in his workshop kneeling beside a penny farthing
A huge penny farthing and a tiny one, surrounded by supporters in London

thumbnail.altText: Dan Bolwell in his workshop kneeling beside a penny farthing
A huge penny farthing and a tiny one, surrounded by supporters in London

items.title: Say goodbye Norma Jean because it's Spicks and Specks quiz time

children.content: Your spelling and general trivia skills are useless here. Settle into some pop culture nostalgia.

image.altText: Adam Hills, Myf Warhurst and Alan Brough stand together smiling in front of a brick wall with Spicks and Specks posters

items.title: Like The Beatles and Beach Boys? You're going to love The Lemon Twigs

children.content: These New York twenty-somethings make music indebted to Brian Wilson, Todd Rundgren and the Fab Four. But do The Lemon Twigs get sick of the comparisons?

image.altText: White brothers with shoulder-length brown hair pose in winter coats against a wooden window frame

items.title: In a secret safe at this luxury house, police struck gold

children.content: Indonesian police investigating suspected corruption discover millions of dollars worth of currency and 74 kilograms of gold in a safe hidden in a luxury house near Jakarta.

gallery.altText: Gold bars on two suitcases pictured from above.

thumbnail.altText: Gold bars on two suitcases pictured from above.

items.title: 'Lack of evidence' means Lao officials won't lay charges over teens' deaths

children.content: Investigators in Laos gave one of their first detailed accounts of the investigation into the deaths of six people, including 19-year-old Australians Holly Morton-Bowles and Bianca Jones, saying they don't have enough evidence to lay charges because autopsies were not carried out.

image.altText: Side-by-side photos of Bianca Jones and Holly Bowles, two Melbourne teens who have been hospitalised in Thailand

items.title: Australia's Herbert makes miracle shot but falls to fourth at The Open

children.content: Australia's Lucas Herbert makes one of the shots of The Open, but loses his day two lead to fall to fourth.

image.altText: A man swings a golf driver.

items.title: How brighter, safer headlights may cause problems for other drivers

children.content: Bright LED headlights improve visibility for motorists and make them significantly safer at night, but what about the driver coming the other way?

gallery.altText: Glare from a new car's headlights at night.

thumbnail.altText: Glare from a new car's headlights at night.

items.title: Fight erupts over 500-fold increase of 'forever chemical' on berries

children.content: A proposal to increase how much pesticide residue is allowed on berries has angered anti-pesticide campaigners, who say the "forever chemical" poses too great a risk.

image.altText: Rinsed, wet blueberries in a metal colander in the sink.

items.title: Ferrari driver dead, woman in 70s injured in Gippsland crash

children.content: The woman, aged in her 70s, was flown to the Alfred Hospital with life-threatening injuries.

image.altText: red car wreckage

items.title: Eight killed, 60 wounded, as Ukraine attacks Russian warehouses

children.content: Two sprawling warehouses of Russia's major online retailer are hit by Ukrainian drones, local officials say, with massive plumes of smoke towering over a facility at Elektrostal, 50 kilometres east of Moscow.

gallery.altText: Two huge plumes of grey smoke are rising from the scene of a warehouse fire.

thumbnail.altText: Two huge plumes of grey smoke are rising from the scene of a warehouse fire.

items.title: New energy scheme adding to bills, SA government admits

children.content: A new SA government energy scheme set to collect almost $44 million from electricity users this financial year has been described by a critic as a "hidden charge".

image.altText: A Stobie pole with powerlines.

items.title: Inquiry hears of CFMEU's political influence and intimidation in Queensland

children.content: The inquiry heard claims former senior public servants and industry figures faced aggressive union tactics.

image.altText: CFMEU's Michael Ravbar (left) and Jade Ingham

items.title: Ex-tradie in the running for Australia's best small bar prize

children.content: Taran Howard opened up his small bar in Sydney's inner west last September. Since then, he has learnt how to mix drinks "on the fly".

gallery.altText: Taran Howard owner of ravenspur small bar in sydney

thumbnail.altText: Taran Howard owner of ravenspur small bar in sydney

items.title: Musos fear payday super rules could ruin live music industry

children.content: Two long-running Australian jazz festivals have been cancelled, with organisers blaming new superannuation rules.

image.altText: a man, holding an instrument, looking at the camera

items.title: 'Not particularly fond of him': McIlroy blasts American over Open drama

children.content: Golf champion Rory McIlroy takes aim at Bryson DeChambeau after the American causes a delay in tee times being released.

image.altText: A golfer looks on during a round.

items.title: 'We didn't give up': Afghan women's refugee cricketers celebrate ICC decision

children.content: Five years since fleeing Afghanistan after the Taliban returned to power, the country's women's refugee cricket team has secured a pathway to compete at the international level.

gallery.altText: Benafsha Hashimi, Afghanistan Women's Cricket Team

thumbnail.altText: Benafsha Hashimi, Afghanistan Women's Cricket Team

items.title: Scot breaks 27yo mile record, Aussies shine at Diamond League

children.content: Scotland's Josh Kerr stuns the crowd by breaking the mile record, as Australia's women shine at the Diamond League meet in London.

image.altText: A man screams in celebration after a race.

items.title: Unstoppable Pogačar claims another Tour de France stage as history calls

children.content: Tadej Pogačar wins stage 14 of the Tour de France and increases his overall lead as a fifth overall victory looms.

image.altText: A man celebrates while riding a bicycle.

items.title: 'Almost certainly new' species of giant earthworms found in SA

children.content: A South Australian ecologist has been tracking giant earthworm sightings for 30 years, despite the species not officially existing in the state.

image.altText: A child holding up a worm which stretches from above his head to his waist

items.title: From 40 cartographers to two, street directories are nearing end of the road

children.content: While sales of the once-ubiquitous street directories have fallen dramatically since their peak in the 1980s, they still have a niche market.

image.altText: A street directory sits on a steering wheel

items.title: Aircraft from 15 countries touch down in Darwin ahead of war games

children.content: Military aircraft and aviators from 20 countries land in Darwin ahead of Exercise Pitch Black 2026, with the biennial war games poised to begin.
